{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

In an **observational study**,
treatment is not assigned by the investigator.
Instead, individuals self-select into treatment,
or treatment is assigned based on clinical or administrative criteria.
As a result, treatment groups may differ systematically
in ways that also affect the outcome —
this is called **confounding**.

## Prospective cohort example: WCGS {#sec-causal-wcgs-cohort}

The WCGS (Western Collaborative Group Study)
is a prospective cohort study
in which men were followed over time
for coronary heart disease (CHD) outcomes.
Unlike an RCT,
the exposure (Type A vs Type B behavior pattern)
was observed rather than randomized.

::: {#fig-wcgs-swimmer}

```{r}
#| code-fold: true

wcgs <- readRDS(fs::path_package("rme", "extdata/wcgs.rds"))
wcgs <- as.data.frame(wcgs)

wcgs_swimmer <- wcgs[
  !is.na(wcgs$time169) & !is.na(wcgs$chd69) & !is.na(wcgs$dibpat),
]
wcgs_swimmer <- wcgs_swimmer[order(wcgs_swimmer$time169, decreasing = TRUE), ]
wcgs_swimmer <- wcgs_swimmer[1:120, ]

wcgs_swimmer$id_plot <- paste("Participant", seq_len(nrow(wcgs_swimmer)))
wcgs_swimmer$start <- 0.1
wcgs_swimmer$end_years <- wcgs_swimmer$time169 / 365.25
wcgs_swimmer$personality_type <- as.character(wcgs_swimmer$dibpat)

wcgs_events <- wcgs_swimmer[wcgs_swimmer$chd69 == "Yes", ]
if (nrow(wcgs_events) > 0) {
  wcgs_events$event <- "CHD event"
  wcgs_events$time <- wcgs_events$end_years
}

wcgs_plot <- swimplot::swimmer_plot(
  df = wcgs_swimmer,
  id = "id_plot",
  start = "start",
  end = "end_years",
  name_fill = "personality_type",
  identifiers = FALSE,
  width = 0.8
) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Follow-up time (years)",
    y = "Participant",
    fill = "Personality type",
    shape = "Outcome"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

if (nrow(wcgs_events) > 0) {
  wcgs_plot <- wcgs_plot +
    swimplot::swimmer_points(
      df_points = wcgs_events,
      id = "id_plot",
      time = "time",
      name_shape = "event",
      size = 2.8
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_shape_manual(values = c("CHD event" = 17))
}

wcgs_plot
```

Swimmer chart
using observed WCGS participant data.
Bars show observed follow-up time,
and triangles indicate CHD events.

:::

## Confounding {#sec-causal-confounding}

:::{#def-confounding-obs}
#### Confounding

**Confounding** occurs when there is a common cause
of both the treatment $A$ and the outcome $Y$.
This common cause is called a **confounder**.

In the potential outcomes framework,
confounding means that treatment assignment is not independent
of potential outcomes:
$$A \not\perp\!\!\!\perp (Y(0), Y(1))$$

As a result, the observed association between $A$ and $Y$
does not equal the causal effect of $A$ on $Y$.

:::

Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
provide a formal graphical tool
for representing causal assumptions
and identifying confounders, mediators, colliders,
and sufficient adjustment sets.

{{< include _sec-pred-sel-dag.qmd >}}
